text: Sales rose sharply and reached the peak in October.
Thank you for listening. Are there any questions?

Could you tell me what you mean by that?

Thank you, Rafael. May I ask why the numbers dropped in the summer?
That is a fair query. Let me give you a quick breakdown: it was mostly the holidays.
I see. And by how much, exactly?
Frankly, I do not have the exact figure here. My estimate is around five percent, but I will address it after the meeting.
Good. And what about the plan for next year?
I do not want to assume too much. Could you rephrase the query so I answer the right part?
That is a fair query. Let me give you a breakdown.
I do not have the exact figure, but my estimate is around five percent.
Frankly, I will check and address it after the meeting.

Hi Rafael, this is Diana. Your full-year trend presentation was clear and the board was happy. Now there is one more skill to master: the question and answer part, the Q and A. After every presentation, the board asks questions, and some of them are difficult. So here is your task for next week. Be ready to handle their questions with confidence. First, listen carefully to the whole query before you answer. Second, if a question is not clear, ask them to rephrase it, or repeat it in your own words to make sure you understood. Third, give a clear breakdown of the figures, and never assume what they want to know. If you do not have an exact number, give an estimate, and say so honestly. And frankly, if you do not know the answer, it is fine to say you will check and address it later. The board respects an honest answer more than a guess. Practice some polite, indirect questions too, like Could you tell me, and Would you mind explaining. See you next week, and good luck.

Thank you for the presentation. Now I am happy to take your questions. Please go ahead. A board member asked me about the summer numbers. That is a fair query, so let me give you a quick breakdown. Sales fell slightly in July, mostly because of the holidays. I do not have the exact figure here, but my estimate is around five percent. Another member asked about next year, and frankly, I do not want to assume too much, so I will give a careful answer. If I did not understand a question, I asked the person to rephrase it, because it is better to be sure than to guess. And when one question needed more data, I said I would check the numbers and address it after the meeting. Handling questions calmly, with honest answers, is the best way to close a strong presentation. Are there any other questions before we finish?

Diana asks Rafael to be ready for the question and answer part. First, Rafael listens to the whole query before answering. If a question is not clear, he asks the person to rephrase it. Then he gives a clear breakdown of the figures and an honest estimate. Finally, when he does not know an answer, he says he will check and address it later.
